an5.99:1.1: “Monks, the lion, the king of beasts, leaves his lair in the evening.
an5.99:1.2: Having left his lair, he stretches himself.
an5.99:1.3: Having stretched himself, he looks all around the four directions.
an5.99:1.4: Having looked all around the four directions, he roars his lion’s roar three times.
an5.99:1.5: Having roared his lion’s roar three times, he heads out for game.
an5.99:1.6: When he gives a blow to an elephant, he gives the blow attentively, not inattentively.
an5.99:1.7: When he gives a blow to a buffalo…
an5.99:1.9: to a leopard, he gives the blow attentively, not inattentively.
an5.99:1.10: When he gives a blow to small animals, even to hares & cats, he gives the blow attentively, not inattentively.
an5.99:1.12: [He thinks,] ‘May the skill I’ve acquired not be lost.’
an5.99:2.1: “‘Lion,’ monks, is a synonym for the Tathāgata, worthy & rightly self-awakened.
an5.99:2.2: When the Tathāgata teaches the Dhamma to his assembly, that is his lion’s roar.
an5.99:2.3: When the Tathāgata teaches the Dhamma to monks, he teaches the Dhamma attentively, not inattentively.
an5.99:2.4: When the Tathāgata teaches the Dhamma to nuns…
an5.99:2.6: to female lay followers, he teaches the Dhamma attentively, not inattentively.
an5.99:2.7: When the Tathāgata teaches the Dhamma to run-of-the-mill people—even to beggars & hunters of fowl—he teaches the Dhamma attentively, not inattentively.
an5.99:2.9: The Tathāgata is one who respects the Dhamma, reveres the Dhamma.”
